teaching.title: std::vector — The C++ Dynamic Array
teaching.analogy: vector is C++'s smart array — it grows automatically, knows its own size, and gives you safety with bounds-checking (.at()). Think of it as a Java ArrayList but with C++ speed and RAII memory management.
teaching.content: vector<T> from <vector>. push_back(x) adds to end. pop_back() removes last. size() returns count. at(i) bounds-checked access. [] is faster but unchecked. Empty: empty(). Clear: clear(). Works with range-based for.

examples.title: vector Operations
examples.explanation: The go-to container for sequences in C++.

examples.title: vector vs array vs Java ArrayList

teaching.funFact: std::vector is the most used C++ container. It's so efficient that Bjarne Stroustrup (C++ creator) said 'When in doubt, use a vector.' Its cache-friendly memory layout makes it faster than linked lists even for insertions in practice! ⚡

teaching.title: std::array — Modern Fixed Arrays
teaching.analogy: std::array is C's raw array wearing a C++ safety vest. Same speed and stack allocation, but now it knows its own size, works with range-based for, at() checks bounds, and integrates with STL algorithms.
teaching.content: array<T,N> requires size at compile time. It's a value type (copyable). Unlike C arrays, it doesn't decay to a pointer mysteriously. Use when size is fixed and known at compile time.

examples.title: std::array

teaching.funFact: std::array is zero-overhead compared to C arrays — the compiler generates identical machine code! But you get the full C++ interface: size(), begin(), end(), at(), and compatibility with all STL algorithms. 🎯

mcq.q: vector<int> v. How do you add element 5 to the end?
mcq.explanation: push_back(): Adds element to the END of the vector. O(1) amortized. Front insertions use insert(v.begin(),x) but are O(n).

options: at(i) throws out_of_range if OOB; [] is undefined behavior
at() is slower for all access
mcq.explanation: Safety: at(i) checks bounds and throws std::out_of_range on invalid index. v[i] has NO check — accessing invalid index is undefined behavior (faster but dangerous).

mcq.q: How does std::array differ from C's int arr[N]?
options: std::array knows its size, works with range-for, at(), STL algorithms — C arr doesn't
mcq.explanation: Better Array: std::array wraps a C array while adding: size(), at() bounds checking, range-based for compatibility, copy semantics, and STL algorithm support.
pop_back: Removes the LAST element. {1,2,3} → pop → {1,2}. size = 2.

mcq.q: Which C++ container is best for most sequential data needs?
mcq.explanation: Use vector: Bjarne Stroustrup's advice — 'when in doubt, use vector.' Cache-friendly, dynamic, works with all algorithms. Default choice for sequences.

coding.title: push_back count
pop_back result
at() vs []
coding.objective: For bounds-checked safe access, use v.___ or v[]?
coding.hint: at(i) throws std::out_of_range on invalid index. [] has no check.

options: v[5] is out of bounds (size=3) — undefined behavior! Use v.at(5) to get an exception, or check size first.
debugging.answer: v[5] is out of bounds (size=3) — undefined behavior! Use v.at(5) to get an exception, or check size first.
debugging.hint: [] doesn't check bounds. Use .at() to catch OOB or ensure index < v.size().

sublevels.title: STD::STRING
sublevels.subtitle: C++ string class — safe and feature-rich

teaching.title: std::string — C++ String Class
teaching.analogy: C++ string is C's char array wearing a full-featured suit of armor. No manual null terminator, no buffer overflows, no strcmp — just clean methods like Java's String but with value semantics and mutable operations.
teaching.content: std::string from <string>. Mutable (unlike Java String). key operations: +/+= concatenation, length()/size(), substr(i,len), find(s), at(i), [], empty(), compare(). Use == for comparison (works correctly!)

examples.title: std::string Operations
examples.explanation: Safer and more powerful than char arrays.

examples.title: C++ string vs C char[]

teaching.funFact: C++ std::string stores short strings (≤15 chars on most implementations) directly in the object without heap allocation — this is the Small String Optimisation (SSO). Longer strings go on the heap. Ultra-fast for short strings! ⚡

mcq.q: C++ string comparison: s1 == s2 compares:
mcq.explanation: Content Compare: C++ std::string overloads == to compare content correctly. Unlike Java String where == compares references, and unlike C where == compares char* addresses.
C++ substr(pos, len): Start at index 1='e', take 3 characters: 'e','l','l'. Result: 'ell'. Note: Java substring(i,j) uses START and END. C++ substr(pos, COUNT).

mcq.q: string s='hello'; s+= ' world'; Is this valid?
options: Yes — C++ strings ARE mutable, += appends
mcq.explanation: Mutable: Unlike Java's String, C++ std::string is MUTABLE. += directly modifies the string in place. No need for StringBuilder equivalent!
string::npos: When find() doesn't find the substring, it returns string::npos (a very large value, typically 18446744073709551615 on 64-bit). Always check: if(s.find('x') != string::npos).

coding.title: C++ string ==
coding.objective: string a='test',b='test'. Does a==b evaluate to true?
coding.hint: C++ string == compares content — both are 'test' so true.

coding.title: substr(pos,len)
string mutable
coding.hint: C++ string is mutable — += appends in place.

options: find() returns string::npos (not -1) when not found. Use: if(word.find('orld') == string::npos)
debugging.answer: find() returns string::npos (not -1) when not found. Use: if(word.find('orld') == string::npos)
debugging.hint: C++ find() returns string::npos (huge unsigned value) when not found, NOT -1.

prediction.explanation: length=9. substr(4,5)='Clash'. find('la') finds at index 5 ≠ npos → true → prints 1 (bool without boolalpha).

teaching.title: std::map & std::unordered_map
teaching.analogy: map is a sorted dictionary — keys always in alphabetical/numerical order. unordered_map is a hash table — blazing fast O(1) lookup but no guaranteed order. Like sorted vs unsorted filing cabinets.
teaching.content: map<K,V> (sorted, O(log n)) and unordered_map<K,V> (hash table, O(1)). Insert with [key]=val or insert(). Access with [key] (creates if missing!) or at(). count(key) checks existence (0 or 1 for map).

examples.title: map and unordered_map
set — Unique values

teaching.funFact: C++17 structured bindings (auto& [k,v]) make map iteration beautiful. Before C++17 you'd write: for(auto& pair : m) { pair.first; pair.second; }. The [k,v] syntax unpacks pairs automatically! 🆕

mcq.q: map<string,int> m; m['x'] for missing key 'x':
mcq.explanation: Creates with 0: map [] operator CREATES the key with a default value (0 for int) if it doesn't exist. Use .count(key) to check existence, or .at() which throws.

mcq.q: map vs unordered_map — which has sorted keys?
mcq.explanation: map: Sorted by key (Red-Black Tree, O(log n)). unordered_map: Hash table (O(1) average), no order. Choose based on whether you need sorted iteration.
Unique: set stores only UNIQUE values. {3,1,2,1,3} → duplicates removed → {1,2,3}. size = 3.

mcq.q: m.count('key') return value for an existing key in map:
mcq.explanation: count(key): For map/set, returns 1 if key exists, 0 if not. (For multimap, returns actual count). Use as boolean: if(m.count('key')) { ... }

coding.title: map access
set size

options: m['mana'] inserts mana=0 if not present. Use m.count('mana')>0 && m.at('mana')>0
debugging.answer: m['mana'] inserts mana=0 if not present. Use m.count('mana')>0 && m.at('mana')>0
debugging.hint: map/unordered_map [] creates the key with default value 0 if missing. Use count() or find() to check existence safely.

prediction.explanation: set stores unique values. 'c++' inserted twice → stored once. {c++, java}. size=2.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE STL ARCHITECT
teaching.analogy: The STL Architect designed the entire C++ Standard Library — vector, string, map, set, and every algorithm. It knows the time complexity of every operation, every gotcha, every subtle difference from Java.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 4 C++: vector push_back/pop_back/size/at/[], std::array size(), string == comparison/substr(pos,len)/find/mutable, map/unordered_map [] creates keys, set uniqueness, count() for existence.

examples.title: Full C++ STL Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ vector: push_back/pop_back/size()/at()
✅ at() throws OOB; [] is undefined behavior
✅ std::array<T,N> — fixed, stack, safe
✅ string: ==content, +=mutable, substr(pos,len)
✅ find() returns npos (not -1) if missing
✅ map: sorted O(log n), [] creates keys!
✅ unordered_map: O(1), no order
✅ set: unique sorted values, count()

teaching.funFact: The STL was designed by Alexander Stepanov using mathematical concepts from abstract algebra. The generic programming paradigm (templates + algorithms + containers) he introduced changed how all modern languages are designed! 🧮

mcq.explanation: at() Safe: v.at(5) checks bounds and throws std::out_of_range. v[5] would be undefined behavior.

mcq.q: C++ string s1='a'; string s2='a'. s1==s2 is:
mcq.explanation: Content Compare: C++ string == compares content. Different from Java (use .equals()) and C (use strcmp). s1==s2 correctly returns true.
substr(pos,len): Start at 2='l', take 3: 'l','l','o'. Result: 'llo'. Wait: H=0,e=1,l=2,l=3,o=4. substr(2,3) = l,l,o = 'llo'. Correct answer: 'llo'.

mcq.q: map<string,int> m; cout << m['x'];. Before this, 'x' doesn't exist. Result?
mcq.explanation: Creates Default: map [] inserts the key with default value (0 for int) if missing. Prints 0. This is a common bug — use count() or find() to check existence.

mcq.q: unordered_map lookup is O(?) average vs map O(?)?
mcq.explanation: Hash vs Tree: unordered_map = O(1) average (hash). map = O(log n) (Red-Black Tree). Use unordered_map for speed, map for sorted iteration.

coding.title: Boss: vector accumulate
Boss: string find

options: auto name copies — modifying name doesn't change names. Use: for(auto& name : names)
debugging.answer: auto name copies — modifying name doesn't change names. Use: for(auto& name : names)
debugging.hint: auto copies each element. auto& creates a reference — use it to modify the original.

prediction.explanation: map iterates in SORTED key order: a,b,c → prints 'abc'.
